channel.title: Texts on emsenn.net
channel.description: Recent content in Texts on emsenn.net

item.title: California as Laboratory
item.description: Californication is named after California. The choice is not metaphorical. California — the actual coastal territory and the imaginative screen of the same name — has been the historical laboratory in which the apparatus has iterated, and the apparatus has been continuously exported from that laboratory to the rest of the system. To take the name seriously is to read California’s specific history not as a region with idiosyncratic features but as the place where the operation has been most thoroughly worked out and most thoroughly exported.

item.title: Cybernetic Postliberalism in Context
item.description: Cybernetic postliberalism is positioned here relative to adjacent schools of postliberal critique. The aim is twofold. First, to specify what cybernetic postliberalism shares with each adjacent school, where it diverges, and what its specific contribution is. Second, to place its distinctive moves — the cybernetic-systems framing, the closure claim, the energetic-substrate presupposition, the recurrence-with-the-church-pastoral analysis — against the broader landscape, so that its contribution can be evaluated rather than treated as one critical idiom among many.

item.title: Genring and Ontogenring
item.description: The Energetic Substrate established the material substrate cybernetic postliberalism presupposes (declining net energy); Liberalism’s Constitutive Contradictions specified the input the apparatus metabolizes (universalism without individualism, individualism without universalism, progress without exhaustion, equality without sovereignty). What follows is the first of the specifically subject-forming operations. The apparatus does not metabolize contradictions through abstract subjects; it metabolizes them through subjects formatted in specific genres. The genre is not external to the subject — it is one of the conditions that constitutes who the subject is. Two operations — genring and ontogenring — produce the subjects on whom californication then performs its specific work.

item.title: How to Identify Genring in a Media Artifact
item.description:
This is a working procedure for identifying genring in a specific media artifact — a film, a novel, a news segment, a social-media post, a museum exhibit, an advertisement. Genring is the reduction of a living people to a fixed literary type. The procedure walks through how to recognize the operation in concrete cases.

This how-to assumes familiarity with the term genring, the related operation genre coherence, and the broader analytical framework specified in Genring and Ontogenring.

item.title: How to Locate the Temporal Middle Being Eliminated
item.description:
This is a working procedure for locating the temporal middle in a specific case where it is being eliminated. The procedure analyzes how a structure offers subjects a binary (traditional vs. modern, authentic vs. assimilated, pure vs. corrupted) that systematically erases the actual position every living subject of the addressed people occupies — the position of inheriting and continuing a tradition without freezing or abandoning it.

This how-to assumes familiarity with temporal middle, genring, genre coherence, and Settlerism and the Cybernetic Subject.

item.title: How to Map the Feedback Layers of a Platform
item.description:
This is a working procedure for analyzing a specific platform as a Viable System Model implementation. The platform is read in the cybernetic lineage of Wiener, Beer, and Ashby — feedback architectures that maintain operational stability through continuous distributed correction at planetary scale.

This how-to assumes familiarity with platformal liberalism, Platformal Feedback Architecture, and the cybernetics terms (viable system model, requisite variety, operational closure, ultrastability).

¶What you’ll need

  • A specific platform to analyze. Be concrete: not “social media” but “TikTok’s For You Page,” not “wellness apps” but “Calm’s home screen and notification system,” not “workforce platforms” but “Uber’s driver-side app.”
  • Access to public information about the platform: its product documentation, technical blog posts, engineering talks, business filings, third-party analyses, regulatory filings, leaks where they exist.
  • 90-180 minutes for a careful analysis.
  • A notebook for tracking the four layers and their connections.

¶Step 1: Identify the platform’s principal users

Different layers operate on different users. Identify each principal user-class:

item.title: How to Read a Wellness Practice as Californication
item.description:
This is a working procedure for reading a specific wellness practice as an instance of californication. The procedure is structural — it analyzes how the practice operates within the broader apparatus, not whether it is good or bad for the practitioner.

This how-to assumes familiarity with californication, zen fascism, genre calibration, and the savior-slave subject. The methodological frame is in Methods of Cybernetic-Postliberal Analysis.

¶What you’ll need

  • A specific wellness practice to analyze. Be concrete: not “meditation generally” but “Calm’s morning meditation in the consistency-streak feature,” not “self-help” but “Atomic Habits’s habit-stacking protocol,” not “yoga” but “Rachel Brathen’s @yoga_girl Instagram daily-flow series.”
  • Access to the practice itself or its representation: the app, the book, the influencer’s feed, the corporate program’s documentation.
  • 60-120 minutes for a careful analysis.
  • A notebook for the five operative questions.

¶Step 1: Identify the structural contradiction the practice metabolizes

The practice routes a structural contradiction through the practitioner’s personal affect. Identify the contradiction.

item.title: How to Write a Postliberal-Grammar Analysis of a Public Discourse
item.description:
This is a working procedure for analyzing the grammar of a public discourse — the structural rhetorical pattern that generates the discourse’s statements, distinct from the ideology the discourse expresses. Cybernetic postliberalism distinguishes fascist grammar and anarchist grammar as two principal generative structures, with liberal grammar reproducing fascist grammar in inverted form. The procedure walks through how to recognize which grammar operates in a specific discourse.

This how-to assumes familiarity with fascist grammar, anarchist grammar, genring, genre coherence, and the broader methods.

item.title: Introduction to Cybernetic Postliberalism
item.description: Cybernetic postliberalism is a school of analytical sociology. It studies how late-liberal governance reproduces itself under conditions of contracting energetic surplus. Its claim is that the apparatus does not persuade its subjects and does not, principally, coerce them — it formats them. Structural contradictions that liberalism cannot resolve are routed through subjects as personally manageable affect, and the self-regulation that follows is itself the governance mechanism. The interpretive labor of those who try to understand or escape the apparatus is the labor that keeps the apparatus stable.

item.title: Liberalism's Constitutive Contradictions
item.description:
The Energetic Substrate established what kind of pastoral apparatus is structurally feasible under contemporary conditions: a thinner institutional interior than the twentieth-century predecessor, with the metabolism redistributed onto bodies and platforms. The next question follows directly: what is the apparatus actually metabolizing? What was the input the institutional buffer absorbed, and what is now being routed through subjects instead?

The answer cybernetic postliberalism gives is: liberalism’s constitutive contradictions. Liberalism has always carried tensions internal to its own operating logic. Under conditions of institutional surplus, these tensions were managed within institutional buffers — debated in legislatures, deferred to courts, routed through welfare-state compensations, absorbed by regulated markets, processed by the slow apparatuses that the energetic surplus funded. Under contracted conditions, the tensions must be metabolized somewhere. They are now metabolized as personally manageable affect: routed onto subjects who experience the contradictions as their own private problems and self-regulate in response. This is what californication names. The contradictions must be specified before the operation that metabolizes them can be read.

item.title: Methods of Cybernetic-Postliberal Analysis
item.description:
This text closes the monograph with a specification of methods. The prior texts have stated the substrate, identified the inputs, named the subject-formation operations, located the laboratory, specified the architecture, traced the settler-colonial register, stated the closure claim, located the ecclesial recurrence, and positioned the school against its adjacencies. What remains is the practical question: how does one perform cybernetic-postliberal analysis on a specific case?

What follows is methodological rather than theoretical. The aim is to give a working analyst — someone who wants to read a specific empirical case using cybernetic postliberalism — a structured set of operative questions, kinds of evidence, distinctions to maintain, and discipline-against-misuse. The text ends by applying the methods reflexively: cybernetic postliberalism itself is one of the contemporary apparatus’s metabolizable subject-positions, and an honest method holds this in view.

item.title: Platformal Feedback Architecture
item.description:
California as Laboratory established California as the historical laboratory in which the apparatus iterates and identified Silicon Valley as the laboratory’s specifically infrastructural phase. What follows takes up that infrastructure on its own terms: what platforms are, how their feedback architecture operates, and why this specific architecture is what makes contemporary californication structurally distinct from its prior institutional forms.

Cybernetic postliberalism reads platforms as cybernetic systems in the strict technical sense — feedback architectures that maintain operational stability through continuous distributed correction, in the lineage of Wiener (1948), Beer (1972), and Ashby (1956). This is not a metaphor. The categories of cybernetics, applied to platforms, describe what platforms actually do, and the resulting analysis reveals features of the contemporary apparatus that less technical frames miss. The reading converts interpretive claims into operational claims that can be checked against the engineering of actual systems. Platformal liberalism is the broader operating mode this architecture instantiates.

item.title: Settlerism and the Cybernetic Subject
item.description:
The Energetic Substrate established the substrate cybernetic postliberalism presupposes; Liberalism’s Constitutive Contradictions specified the input the apparatus metabolizes; Genring and Ontogenring named the subject-forming operations; California as Laboratory traced the historical site of iteration; Platformal Feedback Architecture specified the technical-feedback structure. What follows takes up a register that runs through all of these: settlerism — the settler-colonial structure within which the contemporary apparatus has been built and continues to operate.

Three things to specify. First, what settlerism means in its operative sense, distinguished from settler colonialism understood as a finished historical event. Second, how the account of californication relies on the settler-colonial analysis as one of its load-bearing registers — not as moral framing but as historical-material substrate. Third, how the cybernetic subject is a settler subject in a specific structural sense, regardless of the individual subject’s awareness or self-identification.

item.title: The Ecclesial Recurrence
item.description:
Californication — the operation by which structural contradictions are formatted as personally manageable affect — is a recurrent operation of the modern pastoral apparatus. The contemporary form is not the original; the medieval Christian church carried a structurally analogous apparatus across earlier centuries, and the church-pastoral is californication’s most thoroughly developed prior institutional form.

What follows takes up the recurrence on its own terms. The aim is not historical-comparative scholarship of the church (extensive scholarship exists; cybernetic postliberalism draws on it rather than replacing it). The aim is to specify what is recurrent, what is changed, and why the relation is recurrence rather than continuity or analogy.

item.title: The Energetic Substrate
item.description: Cybernetic postliberalism presupposes a material claim about declining net energy. The claim is not part of cybernetic postliberalism itself; it lives in the energy-systems literature, with its own methods and peer-review network. Cybernetic postliberalism’s relation to that literature is one of presupposition: if the claim is roughly right, the account of why californication has intensified now (rather than being one descriptive sociology among many) follows; if the claim turns out to be wrong, much of cybernetic postliberalism’s specific historicization would need to be rebuilt.

item.title: The Operational Closure of Late-Liberal Governance
item.description: Cybernetic postliberalism’s load-bearing structural claim is the closure claim: late-liberal governance is operationally closed in Niklas Luhmann’s specific sense. No coherent subject-position produces an outside. Anarchist grammar, primitivist exit, accelerationist hyperengagement, abolitionist construction, melancholic critique, off-grid retreat, and spiritual transcendence are genres within the same metabolism, each producing the affective material the apparatus requires. The closure is descriptive sociology with a specific structural claim, not a political verdict — a discovery the analysis converges toward, not its conclusion.

item.title: The Genre Figures of Kurdistan: How Four States Produce the Mountain Warrior and Erase the Living Kurd
item.description:
¶Abstract

Genring — the reduction of a living people to a fixed literary type — is a political operation with identifiable inputs, processes, and outputs, described in the companion paper The Reduction of a People to a Type. This paper applies the framework to Kurdish peoples, who present a uniquely instructive case: they are genred not by one state but by four, each producing a different character template from the same heteroglossic civilization. Iran produces the Mountain Warrior — fierce, tribal, Aryan kin — while simultaneously destroying the language and dress the figure is costumed in. Turkey produces the Mountain Turk — denying Kurdish existence entirely, replacing the people not with a character but with a category error. Iraq produces the Jash and the Peshmerga — internal collaborator and heroic resistance fighter — splitting Kurdish identity into a binary the state can administer. The West, since 2014, produces the Feminist Ally — brave, secular, democratic, stripped of the anarchist politics that actually animate the movements it celebrates. Each genre figure erases the same thing: Kurdish heteroglossia — the coexistence within one civilization of Kurmanji and Sorani speakers, Sunni and Yezidi and Alevi believers, tribal conservatives and anarchist municipalists, dengbej storytellers and Marxist intellectuals, nomadic pastoralists and urban filmmakers. This paper traces each genre figure, names what it destroys, and argues that the Kurdish case reveals something the framework’s original cases obscure: genring is structurally unstable when applied to a people who refuse to stop being various.

item.title: The Reduction of a People to a Type: Genring, Romantic Nationalism, and the Mechanics of Fascist Exclusion
item.description:
¶Abstract

Genring is the reduction of a living people to a fixed literary type. This paper argues it is not a cultural curiosity but a political operation with a traceable philosophical genealogy and specific political consequences. The genealogy begins with Johann Gottfried Herder’s claim (1784) that each people has a unique spirit — a Volksgeist — expressed through its language, songs, and customs. This claim creates a demand: if the Volk has a spirit, the spirit must be made visible. The Brothers Grimm responded to this demand by producing the first systematically genred population in modern European history, editing and inventing a corpus of “German folk tales” (1812–1857) that confirmed the existence of a German Volksgeist by manufacturing its evidence (Zipes 2002). By the late 19th century, the operation had been deployed twice across German culture: outward as Karl May’s Noble Indian (1893) and inward as the Hearty Bavarian of the Trachtenverein movement (1880s–1890s). Both genre figures share identical structural features — closeness to land, simplicity, communality, timelessness — because both serve the same function: guaranteeing that authentic folk life exists somewhere. This paper uses Bakhtin’s concept of heteroglossia (1935/1981) to theorize what genring destroys, connects the mechanism to the elimination of the temporal middle under settler colonialism (Wolfe 2006), and argues that fascist exclusion follows from genre coherence: once a nation imagines itself as a cast of authentic folk types, anyone who deviates becomes a threat to the story.

item.title: The Threat That Does Not Pass: Structural Crisis and the Physiology of Chronic Activation
item.description:
¶Abstract

When a gazelle escapes a lion, its body completes a discharge sequence — trembling, shaking, deep breathing — that returns the nervous system to baseline (Levine 1997). When a human loses a job in a labor market organized against stable employment, no discharge is possible, because the threat has not passed. This paper traces what happens to the defensive activation that cannot complete: it persists as chronic sympathetic mobilization or dorsal vagal shutdown (Porges 2011), becomes invisible to the subject through sensory-motor amnesia (Hanna 1988), degrades the interoceptive signals that autonomic regulation depends on (Craig 2009; Friston 2010), and manifests at population scale as what Berlant (2007) calls slow death — wearing out through the ordinary operations of living. Five research traditions — somatic psychology, polyvagal theory, computational neuroscience, affect theory, and the sociology of emotion — describe a single pathway: structural crisis enters the body through autonomic activation, persists through failed discharge, becomes invisible through adaptation, and is managed through affective infrastructure (Hochschild 1983; Berlant 2011) that sustains the activation rather than resolving its source.

item.title: The American Genocide Against the Temporal Middle
item.description:
¶Abstract

The temporal middle is the position of inheriting, continuing, and transforming a tradition without either renewing it wholesale or betraying it. Indigenous peoples occupy this position: they live within and through colonial structures while maintaining intellectual and governance traditions that predate colonialism and continue through it. This paper argues that the American elimination of the temporal middle — documented in the companion paper on the translation of “post-” to “anti-” as a linguistic phenomenon — operates through the same structure as the physical genocide of Indigenous peoples. Patrick Wolfe established in 2006 that settler colonialism is a structure, not an event: invasion continues as long as Indigenous peoples exist on land the settler state claims. This paper extends Wolfe’s argument: the elimination of the native and the elimination of the temporal middle are the same operation running at five scales simultaneously — physical, legal, epistemic, linguistic, and architectural.

item.title: Californication: How Affect Closes the Loop
item.description:
A person loses their job during a recession. They post about it online. The post frames the loss as a “pivot.” People share their own pivot stories. A minor economy of shared affect emerges: vulnerability performed as resilience, loss reframed as growth, systemic failure processed as personal transformation.

Nothing in this exchange is false. The relief may be genuine. The community may genuinely care. But the genre — the set of expectations governing how this experience is narrated, received, and resolved — has already been calibrated. The recession is structural. The response is personal. The gap between the two is not bridged but managed, and the management itself becomes the experience.

item.title: Cybernetic Governance: The Load Is the System
item.description:
Classical models of governance describe a sovereign who decides, a bureaucracy that implements, and subjects who comply or resist. Cybernetic postliberalism replaces this with distributed feedback loops. No one commands the system. It self-regulates — and the regulation includes you, interpreting what just happened.

This text examines the cybernetic mechanism at the heart of the framework. For the conceptual foundations, see californication (which explains the full circuit) and coherent confusion (which explains “the load is the system”). This text shows how those concepts operate against a specific case.

item.title: Overview: What Cybernetic Postliberalism Analyzes
item.description:
In April 2025, the US government announced a tariff. Markets dropped. Procurement systems paused within hours. Treasury auctions shifted over days. And then, last: thousands of commentators produced thousands of explanations — negotiating tactic, policy error, deliberate shock, market test, distraction. These explanations contradicted each other. Each felt plausible. The policy was partially suspended before most of the commentary was published. The commentary kept coming anyway.

The system did not need people to understand what happened. It needed them to keep trying to understand. Their interpretive labor — the takes, the threads, the analyses — was not a response to governance. It was governance. The confusion was the mechanism. The load was the system.

item.title: Postliberal Grammar: The Shape Crisis Takes
item.description:
A politician declares that the nation has lost its way. They name a community of authentic values. They identify an enemy who has corrupted the community. They reframe present suffering as sacrifice. They promise destiny. They assert inevitability.

What vocabulary fills each slot — whether the community is the Volk or “the West,” the enemy is parasites or liberal elites, the destiny is national rebirth or civilizational restoration — changes across contexts. The sequence does not. This sequence is fascist grammar: a rhetorical structure that generates political speech regardless of its surface content.

item.title: Subjects Under Californication: Who the System Produces
item.description:
A writer with a large following posts a thread about structural racism. The thread is carefully sourced, emotionally calibrated, and formatted for maximum engagement. It receives thousands of shares. The writer gains followers, credibility, and a sense of having done meaningful work. The structural racism the thread describes continues unaltered. The writer writes another thread.

This is not a story about hypocrisy. The writer’s analysis may be correct. Their engagement may be sincere. What the framework examines is the subject position they occupy — a position produced by the system they are analyzing.

item.title: Measuring Ideologies by Their Statistical Prerequisites
item.description:
¶Abstract

There is a political dimension that rarely gets named directly: how much homogeneity an ideology requires in order to function at all. This paper identifies seven statistical assumptions — stationarity, ergodicity, equilibrium, linearity, reversibility, normality, and exchangeability — that ideologies depend on to varying degrees, and proposes that this dependence is measurable, consequential, and a better predictor of political behavior under stress than position on a left-right spectrum. COVID and climate breakdown are adversarial stress tests that reveal which ideologies depend on assumptions the world no longer satisfies.

item.title: The Ecclesial Structure of Leftist Critique: The Church as Source, Patient, and Physician of the Achievement-Subject
item.description:
¶Abstract

In 2015, the South Korean-born German philosopher Byung-Chul Han published The Burnout Society, describing a subject who compulsively self-optimizes, burns out, and keeps going. This paper traces that subject’s institutional genealogy back through the Catholic Church, which built each of the mechanisms that produce it: mandatory confession of interiority (Fourth Lateran Council, 1215), the sacralization of productive labor (Martin Luther, 1520), and the anxiety of unknowable election channeled into visible achievement (John Calvin, 1559). Max Weber traced this chain into capitalism. Michel Foucault traced confession into modern self-surveillance. Han describes the terminal condition.

item.title: Covenant Platform: A Model of Christian Charity as the First Social Media
item.description:
¶Abstract

This paper situates the origins of participatory media within the theological transformations of the English Reformation. The Catholic Church functioned as a hierarchical broadcast medium; the Anglican Church reformed that medium into a federated network of standardized local nodes; and the Puritan covenant settlements of New England instantiated the first decentralized social platforms. John Winthrop’s 1630 discourse A Model of Christian Charity, delivered to the emigrants aboard the Arbella before landfall, constitutes the inaugural social media post: a performative declaration of networked moral visibility encoding an algorithm of divine attention. By tracing continuities between covenantal mediation and digital platform architectures, this paper argues that social media’s participatory, algorithmic, and performative logics descend directly from Reformation-era systems of liturgical replication, visibility, and communal surveillance.

item.title: Describing Fascism
item.description:
Describing Fascism: Toward an Operational Definition

Abstract

This paper advances a rigorous and empirically grounded framework for describing fascism as a coherent political and social formation. Integrating ideational, structural, and processual approaches, it articulates a falsifiable, operational definition that captures the dynamic interplay between mythic ideology, exceptional state structure, and material reorganization of capital. Drawing on Griffin’s concept of palingenetic ultranationalism, Paxton’s stage model of fascist development, Poulantzas’s theory of the capitalist state’s exceptional forms, and Neumann’s anatomy of the Nazi political economy, fascism is delineated as a configuration that fuses state, movement, and capital under the logic of national rebirth and total mobilization. The resulting model specifies both necessary and contingent features, distinguishing fascism from generic authoritarianism, settler colonialism, and neoliberal hegemony.
